question: Medical Question: what are the chances of survival if the brain in not functioning?
patient_info: my grandmother has recently had a triple bypass and has had two grand mal seizures in the past 24 hours since the surgery. the doctors are now saying that her brain is not responding to her body..what are her chances of survival right now, and why is she having seizures? she has never been seizure prone..but is a diabetic.
model_answer: [Loss of brain function - liver disease: MedlinePlus Medical ...](https://medlineplus.gov/ency/article/000302.htm): [This is called hepatic encephalopathy (HE)] Once you have liver damage, episodes of worsening brain function may be triggered by: Loss of body fluids (dehydration) Eating too much protein Low blood potassium or sodium levels Blooding in the intestines, stomach, or food pipe (esophagus) Infections Kidney problems Low oxygen levels in the body Shunt placement or complications Surgery Narcotic pain or sedative medicines Disorders that can appear similar to HE may include: Alcohol intoxication Alcohol withdrawal Bleeding inside the skull ( subdural hematoma ) Brain disorder caused by lack of vitamin B1 ( Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome ) In some cases, HE is a short-term problem that can be corrected. People are often not able to care for themselves because of these symptoms.
gold_answer: that is a difficult question to answer without having more information. typically a person does not just start having seizures without a reason, especially later in life without a history of having seizures. it would suggest that something happened during surgery such as lack of oxygen to the brain and stroke. i'm not exactly sure what the doctors mean when they say that her "brain is not responding to her body", but typically just two grand mal seizures should not cause any significant problems in the brain, unless she was having what are known as "sub-clinical seizures" in which she was still having seizures but was not having any externally observable movements - if that continues for a prolonged period it can begin cause damage.the answer really depends on whether there has been brain damage, and how extensive it is. two seizures in and of themselves does not. hope i have answered your query. let me know if i can assist you further.
sources: Loss of brain function - liver disease: MedlinePlus Medical ...<https://medlineplus.gov/ency/article/000302.htm>

question: Medical Question: what to do if abcess on neck has increased after removing the pus in a koch's tb patient?

model_answer: Buruli ulcer (BU), caused by Mycobacterium ulcerans, is the third most common mycobacterial disease worldwide, after tuberculosis and leprosy. It has been reported that the abcess on the neck has increased after removing the pus in a koch's tb patient.
gold_answer: thanks for your question on healthcare magic. i can understand your concern. no, this is not suggestive of improvement. with treatment, either the size of abscess should remain same or should decrease. but since it is increasing, this is not sign of improvement. sometimes, there is infection with drug resistant tubercular bacteria. in this case, even with routine anti tb drugs, patient is not improving. another cause for this is secondary bacterial infection. sometimes along with tb infection, there is infection with other bacteria. so we have to give other broad spectrum antibiotic along with anti tubercular drugs. so better to consult tb specialist and get done abscess fluid culture and sensitivity report to rule out above mentioned causes. hope i have solved your query. i will be happy to help you further. wishing good health to your mother.
